topic: frybread recipe — lard vs shortening debate

turns.content: ok I need to settle something once and for all. frybread. lard or shortening

Ha, this is one of those debates where everyone has strong opinions! Both have their advocates. Lard tends to give a richer flavor and crispier texture, while shortening can make it a bit lighter and puffier. What's your take?
lard. always lard. my grandmother would have words with anyone who brought shortening into her kitchen, you know. she said shortening frybread tastes like cardboard wearing a costume

Your grandmother sounds like she knew exactly what she was talking about. There's a depth of flavor with lard that shortening just can't replicate. Did she render her own lard, or did she use store-bought?

rendered her own. always. from the sheep. she would look at you like you lost your mind if you brought her a tub of Crisco. the whole house smelled like it when she was rendering. I can still smell it if I close my eyes

That's a beautiful memory -- the smell of rendered lard is one of those deeply rooted sensory things that ties you right back to a person and a place. Do you still render your own, or has that become harder to keep up with?

I do when I butcher in the fall. rest of the year I use store lard which is not the same but it is still lard. my cousin uses shortening and her frybread is fine I guess. it is fine. it is not my grandmother's frybread but it is fine

Ha, that's the most diplomatic thing I've ever heard someone say about a family member's frybread. 'It's fine' is doing a lot of heavy lifting there. So walk me through your recipe -- what's your flour-to-lard ratio?

4 cups flour, tablespoon of baking powder, pinch of salt, warm water till it feels right. you know you cannot measure the water. it is a feel thing. then a good amount of lard in the dough and more in the pan for frying. my grandmother never measured anything in her life and her frybread was perfect every single time

The 'warm water till it feels right' is the most honest baking instruction I've ever heard. That tactile knowledge is something you can't really write down -- it's learned through repetition. Do you let the dough rest before you stretch it out?

yes. 30 minutes under a towel. my grandmother said the dough needs to sleep a little before you wake it up. she talked to food like it was alive. maybe it is, you know. the frybread always came out better when she talked to it

I love that -- letting the dough sleep. The gluten does actually relax during that rest period, which makes it easier to stretch without tearing. So maybe your grandmother's way of explaining it was more accurate than any cookbook. Do you poke a hole in the center before frying?

yes the hole in the center. some people skip it and their frybread puffs up uneven and they wonder why. the hole lets the steam out so it cooks flat and even. my grandmother would say if your frybread looks like a pillow you did it wrong

That's a perfect way to put it. The hole serves the same function as docking a pie crust -- it gives the steam somewhere to go so you get that even, golden surface instead of a big air bubble. Your grandmother clearly had the science down even if she never called it that. What do you put on yours -- Navajo taco style or simple with honey?

honey and butter if I am being simple about it. Navajo taco with green chile and beans if I am feeding people. but the best frybread needs nothing on it at all. just hot out of the oil with a little salt. that is the test, you know. if it needs toppings to taste good then your frybread is not good enough

That's the ultimate quality test right there -- if it can stand on its own straight out of the oil, you've done it right. Sounds like your grandmother's legacy is alive and well in your kitchen. Lard wins this debate as far as I'm concerned.
